segments.text: Morning, David. If you can turn your camera on and your mic on.
We've got 10 minutes-
... with you this morning, including any time for questions. Over to you.
All right. Thank you.
Thank you for the opportunity to talk to the committee on our
submission. So NTVPA, we represent about 2,000
payroll practitioners throughout the country.
I want to cover off a little bit more on each of the kind of four main points that
we made in our submission. Our first one was about making payroll
providers in New Zealand compliant and accountable for the
software that they're providing.
At the present time, there are payroll systems that should not be used in the New
Zealand environment, and they can get away with this because there's no
legislation, there's no certification,
there's no IRD or MBIE checks on any
software, and from our side, we see
issues with this all the time.
So this is one of the things we would like to see built into the act.
It was actually in the process of the prior
review of the Holidays Act, but in the
consultative process, the targeted consultative process that happened October
last year, it was written out, and it was clearly stated that it would not be
covered, and it's not followed through into the bill, so it's been
totally removed from the bill.
And this is a major issue because
simplifying payroll software will
not solve the problem. That's a big misconception from
non-payroll people. Software is written in many different ways,
and
that's impacting on how businesses use the payroll software, and there's no
protection, especially for small business, if they've got, especially
overseas payroll software that's non-compliant for the New Zealand environment.
So we're very concerned that it's not in there.
So at the present time, an employer can be taken to task by labor
inspectorate, an improvement notice, enforceable undertaking, whole range of
steps can be taken. We would like to see the same framework applied to
payroll providers, so if there's an issue of a payroll system, they're asked to
provide evidence to show that they're compliant.
If they're not, an improvement notice can be put on them.
If they don't act on that, an enforceable undertaking is put on them.
So all the things that you would normally do for an employer, you could actually do
for a payroll provider.
Now, when MBIE or a labor inspector is talking to an employer, they're talking to
one employer.
When you're talking about a payroll system, you're talking about a whole industry.
You're talking about hundreds if not thousands of employers that are using that
software, and that's why it's important that the software is
compliant. So that's why it's not just about
one situation, it's talking about software that's used across a whole industry,
and this is why it's important. I really like the idea of name and shame.
You've got banning orders for immigration and bad employers.
By just threatening name and shame, a lot of these payroll providers that
are not compliant would act because they do not want to be put on a
list. They do not want to be out there to be shown that they're non-compliant,
because it's about selling software, and that's a really good way to
quickly get all these payroll systems on track.
But we do need certification, and we do need checks from IR and
MBIE on these systems. They are causing major problems.
So yes, it's legislation. Yes, it's how it's managed, but it's also the software
itself and choices made by payroll providers.
There are payroll providers out there that are saying, "Oh, we don't do this
calculation. We don't need it."
And then because small businesses are not payroll experts,
they see what's on the website that say it's fully compliant with the Holidays Act,
and they buy the software thinking it's all been checked by IR, that's another
misconception, and then they get hooked in and they've spent all
this money, and then they find out the software they're using is incorrect.
So that is a major issue, and that's one part of our submission.
Next one is about, we need a payroll specification document for
any new act. This is more payroll technical,
but we had one when we had the partner labor, when MBIE, the
super ministry was formed. They said, "No, not interested.
Go seek independent advice." And that's what's caused a lot of the mess in the
Holidays Act, is because we've had no real leadership from MBIE.
They duck and dive quite quickly. Any question, it's quite vague.
So we need a payroll specification document written for
payroll that will support payroll providers to provide compliant software,
and that's not what we've got at the moment, and we need it for the new act, and
it's got to be for all stakeholders. We do auditing.
We do technical training. We base everything on a payroll spec.
All right? So we get you the correct information on how a payroll system runs.
That must be part of any new act, and it must be out
before the two-year implementation process, so we can actually build a new
system, not at the other end.
Point three was about remuneration.
That's really good. We fully support any type of process you put into a new act to
fix the problems that we've got now, so we can move forward.
We don't want to be dragged back in the last six years trying to do recalculations,
but there's a real problem that we're seeing right now.
So you're saying there's a potential process in the new act
that employers will be able to use to fix the problems of the past.
We're seeing employers right now making decisions to say, "Oh, we're not going to
do anything now. We know there's problems now.
We'll just wait two years or when the act's actually implemented, and then we'll
use your new process to basically fix the problems." So that
means employees will not be paid, not have their leave
fixed until the new act is implemented in years to come,
and that is wrongSo it's sending the wrong message.
The labor inspectorate needs to send a clear message to all employers,
if they've got a problem now, it must be fixed now, and if it's found
that they didn't act on it when the new act's in place and they're asking for this
new remuneration process to be used,
that will not be
accommodated. And that means potentially a whole range of extra
legislative requirements are put onto that employer, because that is basically
wrong, where an employee should have been paid correctly right now.
Thank you.
There's a big section of our submission, and we honestly ran out of time,
about issues, and we just had to put it in bullet form.
I would probably need another month to write up and put the detail behind it.
This act is, or the bill is actually kind of replacing the
overly complex act we've got now with another set of
rules, calculations, and tests, which will create
another set of complexity. And a lot of it,
it's written for software, but it's not written for end-to-end payroll
processing. That's operational payroll, and there's going to be a lot of
stops and starts when you're trying to process pay.
If I process pay on a Monday, I've got to get that money in the bank by the end of
the day. Some of the ways, this, how the act works, it's sort of like
saying, "I've got to stop. I've got to go and ask a few people.
I've got to do a few things, got to do a few tests." That doesn't work in
operational payroll. So it hasn't really been thought out in
that regard.
So there are problems, and I see it's going to step or create a new set of
problems for payroll going forward.
I say devil in the detail. There's a lot of detail missing from this bill that
payroll would need to feel if it's
workable. And there's a lot missing.
And I'm kind of scared that over the two-year implementation process, that's when
the detail will come. Well, that's when we're building systems, and we can't
keep rebuilding the system from scratch as new things come
up. So a lot of the detail needs to be up front when
the act is actually passed, and that's
one of the issues that we have in regards to how this
act's going to be implemented over a two-year period.
I've kind of pushed it through because I don't know about the 10 minutes for any
questions. So
over to
Evan.
Thanks so much, David. Is there any questions,
Phil or Grant?
No, that was extremely interesting and different
from so many of the submissions we've had, so very helpful. Thanks, David.
Yeah.
Thanks, Grant.
So David, just on the point around a document that comes with the specifications,
that wouldn't be defined though in the act.
Your expectation is that just happens as part of the
implementation of it, or are you expecting that there's
a reference to the requirement for that specifications list in the act?
So at the present time, IR, sorry, I've just come up from the flu.
IR has a specification document that they release for the
1st of April changes.
Yeah.
And that's not part of legislation, but all the legislation feeds into that
document.
We need some sort of guarantee, some sort of
ongoing support from MB that, one, the document's going to
be created-
... and then it's going to have ongoing support as case law, as
other things change, it's updated.
So that means you've got a base document that all payroll providers,
all auditors, anyone can actually go to say, "This is how the
payroll system needs to be built."
So it's an implementation commitment, not a legislative reference,
but take the point. Thank you. Hey, look, really good submission.
Thank you for taking the time to join us this morning.
Members, we are now going to move to our second
submission from The Farm Office Limited.
Good morning. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning, including any time for
submissions, so I'll hand straight over to you.
Hello. So my name is Evelyn Bass. I'm actually a
member of the NZPPA.
So I'm a payroll provider, an outsourced payroll
provider.
I do payroll for farms, gyms, cafes,
and liquor stores,
and
I currently have major problems implementing the Holiday
Act because of the flexible and
varied working arrangements, and
this,
the new Employee Leave Act is basically,
idea is to replace the Holiday Act, makes it less complicated, makes it fair
simple,
and the principle of accruing leave for every hour worked
is great. And then all the limitations
and accepting exceptions surrounding
this by
basically
linking it to agreed hours and rosters and
notional rosters, then create, in my view,
unnecessary complexity for flexible work.
And flexible work is increasing, not decreasing,
and creates unfairness to employees
because they force employees
to create limitations in employment agreements that would
then give employees less leave
than they think they should have.
Because if you accrue leave based solely on hours worked,
it's clear, it's easy, it's fair for both the employers and the
employees. It will give an employee time off based
on the time they actually work,
and they get more leave as they work more.
So if somebody is contracted to work 40 hours a week but
always works 50-They will actually
get 50
hours worth of leave for every week of leave that they take, which means they
get the pay they expect, and they get the leave they expect.
But with these limitations where your agreed
hours are 40, but you work 50, you
create a complexity in the payroll
that makes the payroll very difficult for anyone who's
got employees that really work in a flexible way.
Because
on a farm,
one week they work seven days a week, the next week they
only work four days a week,
and their rosters go over three or four weeks.
They vary it during the season. And there's a lot of
people on farms that actually work as and when they
can. So these are people that are hard to
employ because they've got other commitments.
So they work if and when they can, but they are permanent
employees. But if I try to apply the rules in the
current act to those employees,
it would be impossible.
The only way I can see to apply these rules to employees like
that is that the employer and the employee would
agree every Sunday before payday as to what their agreed
hours are,
and then communicate that to payroll, and payroll will then apply
that to payroll they
process. Otherwise, I cannot see any way to apply the
rules.
Because the roster changes, because yes, of course, there is a roster,
and then people have day off. They have employment leave, bereavement
leave, sick leave, whatever, and the roster changes for
everybody in the business.
And so it basically makes it impossible to say these
are agreed hours because we can't put agreed hours in the
employment agreement.
You have a roster, but the roster changes.
So how
are you going to say these are additional hours, or these are not additional
hours? I simply can't
see a way to make it work, unless the employers
incredibly severely change the way they work.
And the way it would work would be unfair to the employees because
what the employer would do is put agreed hours in employment
agreement, which are the least hours an employee might be expected
to work in a week.
So you might say you work 15 hours a week, but there might
be weeks where they work 50,
but they would only get leave based on the 15 hours.
And I don't think employees would be happy if they only got
15 hours of leave. They have got the compensation of
the leave payment for the other hours, but it doesn't give them
leave, and it doesn't give them a fair payment when they are
on leave.
So that's my main point, that the principle is
great, but the limitations to link it back
to agreed hours
creates a huge problem for any truly flexible work.
Okay. Thank you.
Mm.
Any questions?
So Evelyn, is your view that it would create an
incentive for employers to
scale down
the number of agreed committed hours, and that would have the effect
of casualizing more employment?
Well, basically, these are people that have a permanent job-
Mm
... with flexible hours.
So there are no agreed hours in the current employment agreement at
all.
Mm.
But if you force people to create agreed
hours, then what they would do is put the lowest amount of
hours that people work-
Yep
... as agreed hours, because you don't want to pay people for hours they
don't work.
Mm-hmm.
But that's not fair to the employee.
Mm.
But you don't give the employer much choice than
doing that.
Mm.
And I can give you another example.
I do
payroll for a dairy farm where the son works on the dairy farm,
and the rest of the dairy farm is staffed by students.
They're close to Lincoln University.
So everybody gets paid per hour. They get the living
wage.
Mm.
But their roster changes based on when people can
work every single week.
Mm.
Nobody's got agreed hours in their agreement.
And
basically,
once this act comes in, in the way it is now,
those people will either have to sit down with every employee
every week to then agree on the hours they already worked
to then be able to do the payroll.
Mm.
Or these people would completely have to change the way they run their
farm and fire their student employees.
Mm.
I don't think that's the intended
way the law is supposed to work.
Mm.
So
I just tried to apply the
proposed law to all these situations, and I can't.
Yeah.
It's not possible.
Yeah.
You can't do it.
It's not workable.
Mm.
And to me, it's kind of going against the whole spirit
of the law and the change of the law because-
Mm
... work is becoming more flexible.
People do want more flexibility in their life. They do want to work from home.
They do want to work when they want to work.
And yet, by building this relation
with agreed hours into the law, you basically
recreate the problems that there were with the Holidays Act.
Yeah.
Where anyone who doesn't work regular
hours-gets punished in a way that
you can't find. In the Holidays Act, you couldn't define a week
and you couldn't define a day.
Mm.
In the current law, you can't define agreed hours and additional
hours. I mean, different words, but in
essence, the same problem.
Thank you.
That's really helpful.
Thank you, Evelyn. In your submission, you make the point that
you don't think it's appropriate that
leave is accrued while on parental leave.
Just wanting to confirm that you understand that this bill
removes that provision. Am I understanding your comment correctly?
No. The new bill basically pays people for
accruing leave, paid leave, while they're on parental leave.
But to me, that is a minor point, because I don't deal with that very
often. But the point I'm making now, I deal with it
every single week,
and it's going to make my work impossible in to the sense that
if the
law passes as it is right now, I may seriously
reconsider my career choice by the time it comes in.
Okay.
Grant, quick final question.
Yeah, just very quick. So, I take your point, what you're saying.
But just in terms of the casualization and so forth, I'd
imagine students, most of them, would be quite happy just to
receive the money,
extra money in terms of their holiday pay, as opposed to
necessarily worrying about accrued leave because of the nature of what students
they are in that casualization.
So all these people actually choose to accrue leave instead of
having it paid out.
So, no.
Okay. That's interesting.
I mean, you might think so, but the answer to that currently is no.
Yes, I understand. Thank you.
Okay.
Appreciate that.
Thanks very much, Evelyn. That's all we have time for.
Appreciate you taking the time to submit to us this morning.
We're now moving to the submission from Leave It To
Me Limited. So if we can invite
Sandy to the...
You've frozen.
Can you hear me?
Yeah.
Just froze for a moment.
I might be now unmuted. Hello.
If you could turn your video on, please, so we can see you, that'd be
appreciated.
Yes, I'm just trying to look for where this is. There we go.
Great. We've got 10 minutes with you this morning, including any time for
questions.
Yes. Thank you.
So if you could introduce-
Okay. My name's Sandy Price. I'm a business consultant and
I've got Anne with me. She does a lot of our payroll.
So I run a retail business and we also have worked with quite a wide range of
clients for payroll, as well as a whole lot of other things.
We share a lot of concerns with your previous panelist, so I won't repeat them
because I think she's made some incredibly valuable points.
The main thing is the issue of a lot of people who
start out on casual, and particularly in hospitality, eventually morph
into permanent staff who should be
allowed to accrue leave because the issue is one of how you
actually take your leave, that you actually do get holidays, that is the entire
point, as well as being paid fairly for those holidays.
So people who've gone from being casual with 8% paid then going
on to accruing leave, at the moment, they lose out because of the changes at the
year-end. I think the idea of accruing based on the hours
and
financial value is definitely worthwhile, and it
almost leads to a separate employment category,
because there are a lot of people who do have flexible work for the reasons
outlined by your previous commentator.
Those people might have medical issues. They've got children.
They've got other commitments. They've got multiple jobs.
And they're happy, excuse me, to work in that industry.
But that industry does come with a huge amount of variability in
hours available through seasons and through things
like events.
So,
being able to actually take three out of the four weeks of paid leave
requires us to be able to calculate what actually is
an appropriate time period, often taken in the low season
when the hours are less, but they will have accrued holiday pay at a
higher rate.
So I think it's important for any changes to reflect the fact that you're accruing
leave based on time as well as value.
But when you take your leave, the intention is for people to have effectively
three weeks of paid holiday, plus they can take another week of paid.
And a lot of people that we deal with would prefer to be paid.
When they change that employment type, it actually requires them to
take a pay cut. You're going from 8% pay as you go to
accruing leave, and then they lose 8%.
So being able to accrue it at a point where they will receive that
8% guaranteed at a later date is really important.
The other thing that doesn't seem to have come up, I haven't read all of the
details, but a lot of people are paid in a variety of ways.
You've also got allowances like shift allowances.
You've got overtime. You've got steps that are worked at time and a half.
You've got commission that's payable.
Sometimes this is on top of a minimum wage arrangement, and
sometimes it's in lieu of. You've also got people who get paid bonuses, and it's
not always clear in the employment contract if those bonuses should be attracting
holiday pay. And then you've got others on a piece rate.
So your accrual of paid value has a whole
lot of factors to it. Your accrual of time
is important in terms of taking that leave, so it needs to be taken as
a week,
or an equivalent to a week, because the whole point is that everybody gets four
weeks of leave and supposedly two weeks of sick leave.
So how do you make sure that they actually get their entitlement in time as well
as value? Because you're accruing it on a different basis if you have a
variable workload. So I think perhaps one
solutionWould be a hybrid
employment type because it is a growing thing.
It doesn't mean that employers are taking advantage of employees, because it is
usually of mutual benefit to have a negotiated
roster. So if you're able to accrue leave on the basis
of 8%, as long as you can take your leave,
particularly those who work nearly full-time.
If you're part-time, you're quite happy often to receive the money instead of the
time. But if you're a full-time person, you still need to be able to take that time
for rest and recuperation. So it's really important to be able to work out what
that equivalency is. So that's my main point about the
holidays. The second one is about sick leave.
I do believe it was an oversight in the previous act to allow for
10 days of sick leave. There are a lot of people who have extremely variable
patterns, a lot of part-timers.
You have the situation where someone who works one 10-hour day per
week, for example, who can get 10 weeks of sick leave, and that to me is a
bit of a nonsense. It then makes it an incentive for an
employer to have that person working five 2-hour days in order
for them to get 2 weeks of sick leave, and that creates a whole lot of other
issues. So I think the importance with the sick leave is it does need to be
proportional, and it does need to come back to a two-week equivalent,
not in a set number of days. So there are definitely some challenges.
The software companies have risen to the occasion in many cases, but
most of them don't allow for accurate recording of the
actual days worked or of the roster at the point that it was set.
And that would probably need to change.
We also find that we have to enter into a lot of discussions with employers
about the otherwise working day. For example, someone with a casual
work pattern,
the only time you really know that they're sick is if they were scheduled to work
and didn't. And the other part is with stat days, if they
were not rostered to work a stat day.
But had that been a normal week, it's really obvious from
the pattern that that would have been a stat day and they are eligible.
So that leads to a lot of discussions about the otherwise working day, and I
think that is something that's important to keep, because there is
currently a method of calculation and a recommended
system that will pass if you were audited by
the labor department or whatever the current equivalent is, if you were
getting it wrong. So it is important that employers and
payroll people and employees do have a system that
they can use to calculate those things.
Just one last thing is about the accrual of
parental leave.
I don't see how you can accrue time and value if you're not
actually at work. You may accrue time off, which might be
important if you're a new parent, but if you haven't worked for six months, for
example, then you're not necessarily accruing any actual paid
leave. So how would that work? I think it is a bit of a
nonsense. But you do need to be mindful of the time as well as the
value. The other thing which I'm
particularly concerned about is the idea that casual staff should
get 12.5% of
their
rate of pay from the day that they are employed.
That's on the basis that they would receive their sick pay bundled in
with 8% holidays. That's assuming that they're sick.
Most people don't take any of their sick leave, and yet this
proposal is to pay them for it in advance, and that is actually completely
inappropriate. I think people can accrue leave, but
new employees who are a new factor for a lot of employers,
there's no trust, there's no knowledge in having people able to take all
types of leave from day one, I think is a disincentive for
employers to take on people who potentially might be a risk
to them. So I think it will lead to some unintended consequences in terms
of employer behavior. Thank you. I think that's all I've got.
Thanks very much. We've got time for a couple of quick questions, members.
Anyone got a question?
No.
Sandy, thank you so much for
your presentation this morning and submission. I really appreciate it.
We are going to move on to our next submitter now.
Thank you.
Where we're going to invite,
Annick
to
the virtual seat.
Annick, if you'd like to-
Yes
... kick around-
I'm trying to find a video in here.
Oh, no worry. We'll give you a moment.
Sorry. Here we are.
Oh.
Is that on?
Yes, it looks like it's coming on. There we go.
Oh, excellent.
Thank you.
Good morning. We've got five minutes with you this morning.
So, that includes any time for questions. Over to you.
Thank you. Thank you for
giving me the opportunity to speak.
My name is Annick Masselo, and I'm a professor of law at the University of
Canterbury. I have extensive experience in employment law,
and in the area of gender equality
both in
New Zealand and in Europe.
I wish to raise concerns. So in
contrast to the previous speakers, I wish to raise concern
on the idea of having a pro rata
calculation of sick leave under the Employment Leave
Bill. It's likely to disproportionately impact women,
and by extension, children and other
dependents.
Evidence from New Zealand Ministry for Women shows that
women in Aotearoa, New Zealand, continue to take care of
most of the family's responsibility, and women are more
likely to have primary care for
the unpaid care, and family responsibility.
So, they do take care of children, elderly,
people with disabilityAnd this responsibility
directly affect their ability to participate fully in the
education training and the labor market.
This is reflected by employment pattern.
Data from Statistic New Zealand indicates that about
30% of women work part-time compared to about just
12% of
men.
But
what we see is that those are not choices that are
made individually, but they're shaped by
structural factors, which includes high cost of
childcare in New Zealand, which is one of the most
unaffordable in the OECD country.
In this context, it is important to note that New Zealand does not
have a specific entitlement to care leave, and
that's really in contrast to many other countries.
Sick leave serves a double purpose.
It serves the purpose of taking care of the person who is sick, the
individual who is sick, but also to the employee, but also
for the employee to look after dependents.
And because women are more likely to carry the caregiving,
they're also more likely to need more of the leave.
But because of the pro rata, they will be entitled to
less leave, but will still require more
leave.
So the pro rata system compounds inequalities.
Women,
it creates structural disadvantage.
Those with the greater care responsibility will receive the least
access to leave.
In effect, the bill risk penalizing care work, which we know is
essential for families, communities, and the economy at large.
And without a separate entitlement for care leave, the burden
falls disproportionately on women.
In the current New Zealand context, where pay equity has faced
recent setbacks and child poverty is a pressing
issue, it's critical that the legislation does not further
entrench inequalities. And this bill, as
it is drafted at the moment, does exactly that.
So I respectfully put forward that I think the pro rata
approach to sick leave should be rethought.
And I know people really like this idea of equality,
equality, but it's very formal equality, and also that the committee
should consider the idea of introducing a dedicated sick
leave to ensure more equitable outcome.
Thank you for your time, and I welcome any questions.
Thank you very much. Members, we've got time for a couple of quick questions if
anyone likes to ask a question.
I don't have a question, Annick, but that's a really helpful
perspective. Thank you. And not one we've had from other submitters yet,
so I really value that.
Thank you very much for taking time to submit to us.
We'll move on to our next submission.
I'd like to invite George McGuinness to the virtual table,
please.
Hi, everyone. Thank you for-
Morning, George. We've got five minutes with you this morning, including any time
for questions. Over to you.
Cool. Sweet. Hi, thank you for giving me the opportunity of having an
oral submission this morning. I'm a year 13 student in Auckland, and
based on my experiences with my own family, I researched and wrote a
report for school on the relationship between physically active work and
health outcomes in New Zealand. From that research background and my
interest in law and policy, that's why I decided to submit for this bill.
While I appreciate the work that this bill does, I have two main
concerns for workers in physically active jobs.
My first and main concern is the blanket accrual rate.
The bill applies at 0.0385 hours of sick leave per
standard hour worked uniformly across every industry.
This seems fair, but the idea that a construction worker doing grueling physical
work rain or shine, having the same minimum sick leave entitlement as a worker
sitting at a cushy desk is kind of disconnected from reality.
And the evidence supports this. A 2021 peer-reviewed study published
following over 3,500 workers found that those in the highest physical workload
quartile took 2.1 times more sick leave days than those in the
lowest
physical workload quartile. And that's even after
adjusting for age, gender, and lifestyle.
I posit that the bill should be amended to have a tiered sick leave accrual rate.
This can be administered proportionate to preexisting ACC levy
rate groups to prevent any more hoops from being introduced as this bill aims.
And despite the 2.1 times evidence, I propose that it should
be a very modest 50% rise as an evidence-based starting
point for officials to refine.
One limitation that I acknowledge with this suggestion is that the
classifications are by industry rather than individual role.
But this creates smaller inconsistencies in the current framework that ignores
physically demanding work entirely.
My second concern is the LCP framework.
We've already heard from lots of the earlier panelists today about how the
LCP framework creates issues with workers getting sick leave, so I won't repeat
that. But I think it's worth taking their analysis to emphasize the severe
impact on workers in physically demanding jobs, who are often the ones
who go beyond their contracted hours because their work is dictated by the job.
For example, a builder staying longer because the roof needs to be built
before the rain comes in, or a farmer working between their
hours that they can get their crop sowed.
So for workers in these physically demanding roles, the LCP
mechanism creates a dangerous incentive that is detrimental not only to the
workers, but ultimately costs employers more than it saves
them.Because a worker who is continually incentivized to push
through exhaustion and illness to collect LCP, operating like
dangerous machinery or carrying heavy loads, is an ACC claim waiting to
happen. This worker on ACC for months or even years
loses significant productivity, cost replacement, hiring and training,
and a WorkSafe investigation. I feel like this vastly exceeds
the cost that the worker taking more piecemeal sick leave throughout the year.
Therefore, for workers in WorkSafe classified priority industries or those
on the highest ACC employer levies, I think that the LCP payment should be removed
entirely and replaced with leave accrual on all hours worked.
This change also means that industries like healthcare, public sector,
hospitality, retail, and tourism, who largely drove the Holidays Act reform,
get to keep all the bill's LCP framework, as it doesn't have the same
risks to their worker safety. But finally, even if you don't think
anything I say stands today, at the very least, I believe that an
independent occupational health impact assessment should be commissioned
within two years of commencement,
with disaggregated analysis of impacts on Māori and Pasifika
workers, given their over-representation in physically active worker
groups, and thus their health is exceptionally vulnerable in this
bill.
I think that ultimately today, the bill's simplification of employment law
is great, but over-simplifying risks missing the nuance of how employment is
fundamentally different for people in physically active versus sedentary roles.
Thank you. I'm happy to answer any questions, but I know we're quite short on time

conjunction with accounting and legal advice and other things so that we are making
sure we're making the right payments.
So you don't just plug and play, but the configuring
of the system
tells us that we have to manualize large parts of our
process every time you get into, for this
example I gave you around Anzac Day, something that the system
can't be configured in advance to handle.
Got it.
Any other questions?
Thank you, members.
Thank you for the submission,
Alison. We really appreciate it, and I'm sure we'll see you again.
And members, that concludes our subcommittee
hearings. We'll join for the main committee a little bit later
this morning
